claim: JavaScript Allongé is a book about programming with functions, emphasizing how functions flow into many programming ideas like decorators, methods, delegation, and mixins.
evidence: JavaScript Allongé is a book about _programming with functions_ . It's written in JavaScript, because JavaScript hits the perfect sweet spot of being both widely used, and of having proper first-class functions with lexical scope.

claim: Functions in JavaScript can be used to write software that is simpler, cleaner, and less complicated than alternative approaches that are object-centric or code-centric.
evidence: JavaScript Allongé teaches you how to handle complex code, and it also teaches you how to simplify code without dumbing it down. As a result, _JavaScript Allongé_ is a rich read releasing many of JavaScript's subtleties, much like the Café Allongé beloved by coffee enthusiasts everywhere.

claim: Programming with functions involves working with small, independent entities that compose together to make bigger programs.
evidence: Thus, the focus on things like writing decorators. As noted above, JavaScript was chosen as the language for Allongé because it hit a sweet spot of having a large audience of programmers and having certain language features that happen to work well with this style of programming.

claim: ECMAScript 2015 introduced features that make programming with functions easier and more expressive, such as block-structured variables and rest parameters.
evidence: ECMAScript 2015 (formerly called ECMAScript 6 or "ES6"), is ushering in a very large number of improvements to the way programmers can write small, powerful components and combine them into larger, fully featured programs.

claim: Prior to ECMAScript 2015, JavaScript lacked block-structured variables, so programmers used IIFEs to simulate local scoping.
evidence: Prior to ECMAScript 2015, JavaScript did not support block-structuring, so programmers borrowed a trick from the Scheme programming language, and would write: var i; for (i = 0; i < array.length; ++i) { (function (i) { // ...})(i) }

claim: Before ECMAScript 2015, JavaScript did not support collecting a variable number of arguments into a parameter, so programmers had to use workarounds like slicing arguments.
evidence: Prior to ECMAScript 2015, JavaScript did not support collecting a variable number of arguments into a parameter, so programmers would take advantage of an awkward work-around and write things like: function foo () { var first = arguments[0], rest = [].slice.call(arguments, 1); // ... }

claim: ECMAScript 2015 allows for cleaner syntax for block-scoped variables using 'let' and for collecting remaining arguments using the spread operator (...rest).
evidence: We can also write: function foo (first, ...rest) { // ... } And presto, rest collects the rest of the arguments without a lot of malarky involving slicing arguments.

claim: The 'Six' edition of JavaScript Allongé introduces classes, mixins, private properties with symbols, iterators, and generators while maintaining focus on composing entities with functions.
evidence: Thus, the "six" edition introduces classes and mixins. It introduces the notion of implementing private properties with symbols. It introduces iterators and generators. But the common thread that runs through all these things is that since they are all simple objects and simple functions, we can use the same set of "programming with functions" techniques to build programs by composing small, flexible, and decoupled entities.

claim: JavaScript Allongé is not prescriptive about programming techniques, acknowledging that software development choices depend on social considerations, requirements, consistency, and tooling.
evidence: But while JavaScript Allongé attempts to be provocative, it is not _prescriptive_ . There is absolutely no suggestion that any of the techniques shown here are the only way to do something, the best way, or even an acceptable way to write programs that are intended to be used, read, and maintained by others.

claim: JavaScript Allongé focuses on fundamental programming ideas and how they combine to form new ideas, aiming to improve the way we think about programs.
evidence: JavaScript Allongé is a book about programming with functions. From functions flow many ideas, from decorators to methods to delegation to mixins, and onwards in so many fruitful directions. The focus in this book on the underlying ideas, what we might call the fundamentals, and how they combine to form new ideas. The intention is to improve the way we think about programs.

claim: JavaScript Allongé emphasizes teaching the 'why' and 'when' of programming techniques rather than just the 'how'.
evidence: It can focus on the _why_ to do something and _when_ to do it, instead of on the how to make it work

claim: The book introduces new aspects of programming with functions in each chapter, with code examples that emphasize exposition rather than serving as everyday patterns.
evidence: _JavaScript Allongé_ introduces new aspects of programming with functions in each chapter, explaining exactly how JavaScript works. Code examples within each chapter are small and emphasize exposition rather than serving as patterns for everyday use.

claim: The book contains recipes that demonstrate practical applications of the chapter's ideas, drawing upon any aspect of the JavaScript programming language.
evidence: Following some of the chapters are a series of recipes designed to show the application of the chapter's ideas in practical form. While the content of each chapter builds naturally on what was discussed in the previous chapter, the recipes may draw upon any aspect of the JavaScript programming language.

claim: Pure functions contain no free variables and always behave consistently

That emphasizes one of the things JavaScript gets really, really right: Functions as "first class entities." Naming things is a critical part of programming, but all we've seen so far is how to name arguments.

claim: Named function expressions allow a function to have an internal name distinct from the identifier it is bound to.

claim: When a variable is bound with const in an inner scope, it shadows the outer binding without affecting it.
evidence: We say that when we bind a variable using a parameter inside another binding, the inner binding _shadows_ the outer binding. It has effect inside its own scope, but does not affect the binding in the enclosing scope.

claim: Variables bound with const in nested blocks shadow outer bindings, ensuring proper lexical scoping.
evidence: Ah! const statements don't just shadow values bound within the environments created by functions, they shadow values bound within environments created by blocks!

claim: The const keyword allows binding values to names within blocks without incurring function invocation overhead.
evidence: The const keyword introduces one or more bindings in the block that encloses it. It doesn't incur the cost of a function invocation.

claim: Multiple constants can be declared in a single const statement separated by commas.
evidence: We can bind more than one name-value pair by separating them with commas. For readability, most people put one binding per line:

claim: Constants declared with const cannot be reassigned after initial binding.
evidence: JavaScript does not permit us to rebind a name that has been bound with const.

claim: Blocks can contain const declarations and these create new scopes for the declared variables.
evidence: const statements occur inside blocks, we can't use them when we write a fat arrow that has an expression as its body.

claim: If statements can contain blocks which themselves can contain const declarations.
evidence: One of the places you can find blocks is in an if statement. In JavaScript, an if statement looks like this: (n) => { const even = (x) => { if (x === 0) return true; else return !even(x - 1); } return even(n) }

claim: Functions are expressions and can be bound to identifiers just like other values.
evidence: Functions are values that can be bound to names like any other value, passed as arguments, returned from other functions, and so forth.

claim: Every value in JavaScript is either truthy or falsy, and logical operators operate on truthiness rather than strict boolean values.
evidence: In JavaScript, there is a notion of "truthiness." Every value is either "truthy" or "falsy." Obviously, false is falsy. So are null and undefined, values that semantically represent "no value." NaN is falsy, a value representing the result of a calculation that is not a number. And there are more: 0 is falsy, a value representing "none of something." The empty string, '' is falsy, a value representing having no characters.

claim: The ternary operator is an expression that evaluates to either the second or third operand based on the truthiness of the first operand.
evidence: JavaScript inherited an operator from the C family of languages, the _ternary_ operator. It's the only operator that takes _three_ arguments. It looks like this: first ? second : third. It evaluates first, and if first is "truthy", it evaluates second and that is its value. If first is not truthy, it evaluates third and that is its value.

claim: The logical NOT operator (!) always returns true or false based on the truthiness of its argument.
evidence: ! is the simplest. It always returns false if its argument is truthy, and true is its argument is not truthy:

claim: The logical AND (&&) and OR (||) operators do not necessarily return true or false, but instead return the value of one of their operands based on truthiness.
evidence: && and || do not necessarily evaluate to true or false. To be precise: - && evaluates its left-hand expression. - If its left-hand expression evaluates to something falsy, && returns the value of its lefthand expression without evaluating its right-hand expression. - If its left-hand expression evaluates to something truthy, && evaluates its right-hand expression and returns the value of the right-hand expression. - || evaluates its left-hand expression. - If its left-hand expression evaluates to something truthy, || returns the value of its lefthand expression without evaluating its right-hand expression. - If its left-hand expression evaluates to something false, || evaluates its right-hand expression and returns the value of the right-hand expression.

claim: The logical AND (&&) and OR (||) operators have short-circuit semantics, meaning they may not evaluate the right-hand side depending on the left-hand side.
evidence: If we look at our examples above, we see that when we pass true and false to && and ||, we do indeed get true or false as a result. But when we pass other values, we no longer get true or false: 1 || 2 _//=> 1_ null && undefined _//=> null_ undefined && null _//=> undefined

claim: The logical AND (&&) and OR (||) operators are control-flow operators, not logical operators in the traditional sense.
evidence: We've seen the ternary operator: It is a _control-flow_ operator, not a logical operator. The same is true of && and ||. Consider this tail-recursive function that determines whether a positive integer is even: For example: 75 Picking the Bean: Choice and Truthiness const even = (n) => n === 0 || (n !== 1 && even(n - 2))

claim: Function parameters are eagerly evaluated, which can cause issues with control-flow operators that should defer evaluation.
evidence: In contrast to the behaviour of the ternary operator, ||, and &&, function parameters are always : _eagerly evaluated_

claim: To implement control-flow semantics in functions, pass functions instead of expressions to delay evaluation.
evidence: If we need to have functions with control-flow semantics, we can pass anonymous functions. We obviously don't need anything like this for or and and, but to demonstrate the technique: const or = (a, b) => a() || b() const and = (a, b) => a() && b() const even = (n) => or(() => n === 0, () => and(() => n !== 1, () => even(n - 2))) even(7) _//=> false_

claim: Array literals in JavaScript are expressions that create new, distinct arrays each time they are evaluated.
evidence: Array literals are expressions, and arrays are _reference types_ . We can see that each time an array literal is evaluated, we get a new, distinct array, even if it contains the exact same elements:
